text: If I understand correctly, then the generator has 4 segments with 9 coils of copper wire in each segment, a total of 36 coils. And a diode bridge at the output of each coil (a total of 36 diode bridges)? and they are all connected in parallel at the output of the diode bridges for direct current? Or are there 4 diode bridges, at the output of each segment, and they are connected in parallel? How many magnetic poles are there in total. I also planned 4 segments with coils connected in parallel via diode bridges (I have not decided on the number of coils yet). I plan to make the number of poles a multiple of 4 (minimum 4), and connect the segments through the controller: 1) all segments in series, 2) 2 in parallel and then in series in pairs (2 + 2), 3) all segments in parallel, depending on the output voltage from the segment. author: @fredflintstone8048
text:
Bottom line, this video series spends more time focusing on the trivial details of the build rather than getting to the real meat of any 'free energy', or 'green energy' project (often they are very similar) which is the actual power production. This thing will never produce 1/10th of a KWh let alone 1KWh, and the author dances all around the facts about the output as all the free and green energy salesmen do.
The title of the series is a clickbait lie, and reasoning that commercial makers of generators lie about their production numbers so it's OK for him to lie as well is Ludacris . Contrary to that red herring claim I have a 450KWh Onan generator that actually does produce 450KWh. I've tested it under load. 
I'm sorry but I cannot respect any man who says that it's OK for him to lie because everyone else is lying as well.
The proof is in the pudding and the pudding is never put on display or tasted. To say, 'it works', isn't good enough.  In an earlier video numbers of 28-30 volts and 1500ma were tossed out to us.. Giving those numbers the benefit of the doubt because there was NO PROOF PROVIDED, that equates to 45Wh, or 0.045Kwh . 4.5% of the claim of 1KWh.
A far cry from the claim.

author: @moking8095
text: Of all the differing generators, pick the most inefficient type to aim for 1000W. The only efficient type is what is used on the superwind farm generators and they are generating high voltage which can be easily transformed and made to traverse great distances with minimal loss due to resistance. The only way to generate 1000 w is to firstly use the neomidium iron boron magnets, not the weak ceramic type. The windings need to be a continuous run in and out sinusoidally serially through all the magnets with something like 500 volts output then transforming to the desired voltage. This method keeps the operating voltage high and the current low reducing the losses to a minimum. You were on a sticky wicket right from the start.

author: @5Iron
text: First - I have not seen every second of t his series, nor  read every comment. But let me throw this in;  It looks like this design is *possibly*  throwing away more than 50% of the available energy, before any losses in the mechanics or the electronics. Considering the wind coming from one direction, the blades on one side are capturing wind inside the 'cup' of the curve, creating rotation, while on the other side the blades are blocking the wind flow , fighting the rotation. Even with *no* blocking the wind would only be harnessing energy on one half of the available facing surface area.  My apologies if I'm misinterpreting the design. (If you wish to provide an email address I can provide a sketch that might make this clearer. )

text: Robert, Sir. I feel that I should point out the when you are using a "variable power source" (aka a wind generator) to charge any battery that you should really consider a charge controller between the battery and the WG! charging without the Charge Controller can definitely damage the battery due to inadvertant overcharge! Decent CC's are available on the internet, but be very careful about the source and ability to obtain service, advice and heaven forbid the requirement to get a return due to faulty product (the Dhinese made CC's are either good or they are total junk! I know, been there, done that! Keep up sthe good work! PS: reasonably prices Farad capacitors with a reasonable voltage tolerance are more and more available. Check with the auto audio seller! I am currently (bad pun) using two on my totally off-grid solar power system.

author: @JonhsonJones
text:
The project is interesting and the turbine well built, but there are some concepts that are not taken into account in the explanation and drive misconception about wind power production.
1- the curve is theoretical. No actual turbines show a Betz efficiency. Typical efficiencies range from 0.15 for Savonius VAWT to. 4 for highly efficient Darrieus VAWT. The turbine in the video is a derivation of Savonius.
2 - the reason for the lower efficiency is due  to the aerodynamic phenomena around the blades and to the charge controller (mppt) settings. Look for Paraschioviou's books on turbine performance modeling
3 - the biggest error people do buying a wind turbine is to rely on the nominal power value. What has financial value are the Watt per hour, and this is obtained by combining the real wind turbine power curve (not the theoretical one) with the annual wind speed Weibull distribution of the installation site. This is the major reason for client's unsatisfaction when installing a wind turbine.
I find noble the attempt to make wind turbines easier to understand, but caution shall be applied to oversimplification that may undermine confidence of customers and people in general in the technology.

author: @dwrobotics2180
text: Nice project, but I just can’t see it generating that much usable energy. Vawts are generally not very efficient and the type you are working with is not known for high efficiency. I think you would need to be in a tornado for it make 1.2 kilowatts. If you train a fan onto one side of it you are concentrating an unusually consistent and fast air flow directly onto your blades but doesn’t account for drag on the leading edges coming back into the wind. In real world conditions if you had the same airflow but multiplied across the top of your house you really would be in hurricane or tornado. That being said, yours will definitely spin. I’ve seen those ‘quiet revolutions’ turbines all round London and never once seen them spin.😂

author: @sobeflyer
text: Thank you Robert for sharing your time and efforts on this project.  I have two thoughts about improving the design from the aerodynamic end....  First, the cost of the material for the blades is very low, and doubling the length of the blades would double the area of wind energy collected with no more effort or materials at the top or bottom where all the electronics and mechanics are.  Second, the baffles can be increased in number and rotated counter clockwise maybe 30 degrees to bias the incoming wind to improve the efficiency of the blades collecting the energy.   Adding and canting baffles would increase the wind pressure on the inside of the blades, while decreasing the resistance wind pressure on the outside of the blades.  You would be in effect creating an aerodynamic stator on the outside of your aerodynamic rotor.  Think of it this way... you actually have the mating of an aerodynamic rotor/stator pair with an electrical rotor/stator pair.

text:
Congrats Rob!
I understand that many people can offer advice on how to improve something after someone else has done the ground work and that is great. Any viable improvements simply make the whole project more successful. However, lets stay focussed here on simple economics. Once you have built a unit, at whatever final cost to the individual. With virtually no operating costs of the unit, and no doubt very little maintenance moving forward, you will generate enough electricity to pay off the cost to build. Invest some time and effort and a little bit of cash for an indefinite period of reward. That's great economics if you ask me!
I cannot afford a commercial wind turbine, but I can copy Rob's work and afford, in my case, a couple of hundred Aussie dollars.

author: @pierredelport7187
text: Hi Robert. Great project. I started following your channel after I discovered it sometime last year during lockdown while on YouTube and as I also like to salvage parts from thrown away stuff, I liked the way you also do the same and the nice way you share those ideas. I have dozens of boxes full of spare parts of everything in my garage and also like to build something of it.  I can also share your feelings of welding and although I have been welding since 1975, I still struggle as I am not a professional welder and only weld every other month or so. Every time it takes me a few welds before I can weld reasonable again. I wanted to build a VAWT now for a while and do like your latest design. I personally feel the structure as well as the magnets and coils are definitely the best option when you want to get the best system from salvage parts. I am however concerned about losses in your system, not mechanical as your design looks like it runs very smoothly and with minimum mechanical interference. My concern is with the electrical losses in the diode bridges rectifiers. If you did use the cheap ones, you will have +/- 1.4 volt loss with each diode bridge. I do not know what voltage is generated per coil and if it is high, then the 1.4V loss could be insignificant. If the generated voltage is however low, then the losses per coil could have a huge impact on the power generation of the unit as the same losses are per coil and the total loss is the sum of the losses of all the coils. It could be beneficial to investigate into some other method to interconnect the coils in some other configuration and get a three phase output, which can then be rectified. This will end with a higher voltage and also a lower loss over the diodes as there are now less diodes . It can however impact on the cost as the diodes can be more expensive as their ratings will be higher, but there are still salvage parts available somewhere. You will use only 6 diodes for three phase rectification, where you now use four diodes per coil. Anyway, just a thought from my side and keep on with the brilliant channel and work.

author: @idahobob
text:
Mechanically it is looking very good.

With the bridge rectifiers on the coils, they will stop back-flow, so you don't need the diode on the filter cap.
Also, with that many coils, I'm willing to bet that if you put a scope on the output, the ripple will be so low, you don't need the output cap, especially the few uF of a microwave cap.

So just connect the output of bridge rectifiers directly to the battery for the test. It will work fine.

In a more permanent setup, , the main problem is runaway at high winds. You'll eventually will need a dump load or a mechanical way of stopping it. 

Last, if you are expecting something around 2KW, then at @ 12V, that's 166A! In your final setup you'll have to get much more beefy bridges than you have (and think about fusing... maybe just small sacrificial hookup wire to the coils?)

author: @DeliciousDeBlair
text:
Lest people forget, there are ways to passively mechanically enhance the collected wind energy [within reasonable limits, of course] by using funneling type devices, although the specific design here, due to the vertical bar being a bit close to the side of the rotor sort of limits this.

If one can extend the distance of the vertical support framework radially away from the drum, then one can easily add a wind directed funnel mechanism which will increase the cross section of wind striking the rotor, and this funnel, if built properly, should represent Bernoulli's ideal venturi form in one direction [vertically] while being straight up and down the entire length of the rotor, with end plates at the top and bottom which can be either perfectly flat, or a bit tapered, depends on the space you left for yourself around the rotor, and the materials you have on hand to build it with, but generally, it need not be a very heavy material, simply strong and [preferably] light weight.

Since this funnel literally shares the same axis as the drum, one can easily mount it on the top to the same axle, and since it need not be able to spin rapidly, [in fact, the point of it is to simply and reliably remain aimed into the wind, and NOT spinning like the rotor], one can use some kind of small, wheeled bearings to rest it on, and, it does not need to extend under the rotor, therefore  need not be made in any such way as to interfere with the coils...

Another approach, however, could be to actually mount the coils inside of the funnel/air-box, and then use a slipper shaft to extract the power from the coils by simply putting 2 round conductive contacts between the base of the funnel and the fixed base of the complete mechanism, where brushes or conductive rollers can connect these 2 concentric rings to the external circuitry.

This arrangement, while a bit more complex than a stationary set of coils, would allow one to have a freely rotatable wind vane directed air diverter funnel/box which can always remain pointed into the wind, and still allow the narrowest gap between coils and magnets, AND assure the maximum wind utilization efficiency. 

If you need simple sketches of how this mechanism works and a rough idea how it would fit, feel free to message me and I shall sketch out and publish a simple sketch on my DeviantArt profile.

replies.author: @1967spark
replies.text:
@GofuKyersen While it is true that the energy harvested is a "bonus", realistic expectations of the likely amount of that "harvest" are needed for anyone planning on replicating the project. £50 will buy about 250kWh while a project of this scale may take about five years to generate that much. 

Robert has spent a lot of time, effort and material developing his fan, endeavouring to make it mechanically efficient in converting wind energy into a rotary machine.  Whether Robert is interested in following up on the actual "harvesting" turning that rotary energy into electrical energy more efficiently is up to him. (Or anyone that decides to build their own).

I do believe that there are applications for small generators like this, though not as a means of topping up the supply for a building already connected to the grid but as a way of providing power in remote locations for farms etc. (Or even at the end of a long driveway if you needed an automatic gate that operates just a few times a day)

But no matter what the application, an appreciation for the electrical losses is needed and especially if someone wants to just experiment with different coils, capacitors, batteries etc. instead of calculating theoretically then they will have to find some method of measuring how successful they may be, as it's not as obvious as watching the fan turn.

replies.author: @1967spark
replies.text:
I suspect that is being far too optimistic. The average UK wind speed is just less than 10mph which in terms of wind power for a turbine of that size equates to less than 14W. Just 122kWh, before any losses so it would be half that or even less. (but it is debatable whether average wind speed equates to average power produced) . 

Not saying it's totally insignificant, it would perhaps run the LED equivalent of a 100W filament lamp (say 8-10W), maybe more as it would be generating power even when a light isn't needed.

While perhaps it would be uneconomical compared to the national grid supply, I would be fairly certain that there are applications such as remote farm buildings etc. where a grid supply is not practicable.

replies.author: @garethb5729
replies.text:
@karlmyers6518 
316L grade stainless works best. 
Car. 
The ignition source occurs a few degrees before dead center for standard fuel. 
Change this timing too a few degrees past dead center. 

Torch. 
About an egg cup size of HHO sounds like a shotgun at point blank range. 
Keep your bubbler small. Make it on demand, don't store it. And pay extra attention on your flashback arrestor.  Tbh I haven't spent much time on torches, far too scary for me. 

Heating. 
Pump the HHO through a catalytic converter and it recombines into water. Red hot with no flame.

replies.author: @hempev
replies.text: That's 45mph - by then it's not output that worries you, it's survival!

replies.author: @gravelydon7072
replies.text: @hempev We have summer thunderstorms that produce winds stronger than that.  I've driven in winds higher than that. When winds get above 100 is when we start getting worried. The shingles are only rated for 120MPH.

replies.author: @hempev
replies.text: @gravelydon7072 Big commercial units have survival ratings from 40-72m/s, and some off-shore units are rated to 80m/s (that's 180mph), but a typical wind farm **stops** all rotating mass for safety at +/-60mph (wind turbines have been known to catch on fire due to over-speed braking). Anything that weighs less than a grown man shouldn't even be running *that* high.

replies.author: @gravelydon7072
replies.text: @hempev I was in a government built building that had a door rated for 180 MPH +10%. Hate to say it but that door failed early on. We had evidence of tornado strikes on our home but the reason we lost shingles was the flying sheet metal from a neighbors shed roof in that same storm. The eye wall was recorded as at 155 MPH sustained. Our home was dead center of that storm and one of the few surviving power poles in the area is out front of our house. It was put in when our house was built over 40 years ago. Cat 5 storms are no fun and I've survived two so far. One Atlantic storm and one Pacific storm. I'd just as not see a third one. Our home is hardened enough that when Hurricane Irma came thru, we couldn't even claim enough damage to get paid by the insurance company. The local power companies shut down the local nuke power plants when the winds reach 55 MPH sustained. The grid stays up on power generated outside the area that is affected and they keep the natural gas powered ones up till winds hit 100 MPH. Haven't heard at what point they shut down the solar units but my guess is at 100 MPH also so as to protect the grid. Most of our main powerlines ( 3 phase ) are on concrete poles now but Andrew even shattered ones that were two ft across.

replies.author: @hempev
replies.text: @gravelydon7072 Life in Hurricane Alley!! Add to it climate change... We get the most destruction from earthquakes, and they're years apart.

author: @headlessxp4948
text: Really nice to see that turbine finally seeing some use. I would suggest to remove your "anti-backflow" diode though, as its functionality is already included with the full bridge rectifiers and its just eating up precious voltage at that point. Btw, did you use schottky (0.4V DD) or just regular pn Diode Bridge rectifiers(0.7 DD)?

replies.author: @hanslepoeter5167
replies.text:
I agree. Anti backflow diode is not needed. Magnets seem pretty evenly spaced this time. Spacing coils on phase even distances, put them in series and only then use a bridge rectifier might do and would save the power lost on all individual bridge rectifiers.
I'm afraid the power target will not be met by far. Losing the cores and solid oven transformers was a bad idea in that respect. No matter what, it's a lot of fun.

replies.author: @gravelydon7072
replies.text: Diode is needed so as to not keep draining the battery into the capacitor when the turbine is not producing power.

replies.author: @headlessxp4948
replies.text: @gravelydon7072 Yeah sure but we are talking about something in the range of microamperes for the leakage current of the cap. If you are willing to forget about those microamperes, the capacitor just takes power to charge. Once its charged, it acts like an open circuit.

replies.author: @gravelydon7072
replies.text: @headlessxp4948 Even multi Farad capacitors have a discharge rate of under two weeks so the mA loss does add up. Maxell 2.7V 5F super capacitors will self discharge in 10 days as an example and may be under usable Voltage in less than a week. Discharge rates per one person's test showed from 5% overnight up to 50% in under an hour. So a blocking diode can help and as Robert is using used capacitors, we have no idea how they fall in that range of rates.

replies.author: @headlessxp4948
replies.text: @gravelydon7072 But we are talking about HV capacitors in the 10s of uF or even less and rated for more than 2kV and 10MOhms leakage resistance. Also, if those were to develop the kind of leakage you are talking about, they would dissipate about 0.003 ("the mA loss" -> i will be generous and say 3mA) * 2000V = 6W. At 6W of heat pumped into it, that cap would blow up after no time. This means that even at 2kV, their leakage current has to be lower than what you said ("the mA loss"). And since Rob is using those caps at 12V, the leakage current will be basically 0.

author: @wibblywobblyidiotvision
text:
That's looking pretty damn slick.

If you're interested in finding what its actual efficiency is, all you really need, I'd have thought, is a big old resistive load current sink, like a toaster, kettle, or radiant heater coil, hooked up directly to the output, in a relatively steady wind, and measure what it's capable of pushing through it.  No, that's not a realistic usage setup, but it should be able to give you an idea of what its actual output is for a given windspeed as measured with your handheld anemometer.   Get a few data points, compare to your theoretical graph, and extrapolate if possible.

My guess is that you're not gonna be doing too badly, and like you've said all along, it's not really peak power that's important, but what you can pull out at low speeds, but that's still impacted quite heavily by the (electro) mechanical efficiency of your setup.

@evaluateanalysis7974 Exactly , it's a non profit con if you know what I mean . He does not even realise the ramifications of wind speed in the output calculation of a wind turbine specifically the velocity cubed component . 
Pwind = 0.5 * ρ * v³ * A

where:

    A is the sweep area

    ρ is the air density, assumed to be 1.225 kg/m³ by default (you can change it in advanced mode)

    v is the wind speed - the typical usable range is approximately 3-25 m/s
